meta.title: feat: add buildGreeting/shout utility (external Action proof)

overview.lead: Two new utility modules provide string-formatting helpers for greeting and farewell messages, each with built-in argument validation and straightforward output logic.

bullets.title: New buildFarewell utility
bullets.body: src/farewell.mjs defines buildFarewell(name), which checks whether 'name' is a non-empty string and throws an error for invalid input. If valid, returns a standardized farewell message.

bullets.title: Greeting and shout helpers added
bullets.body: src/greeting.mjs provides buildGreeting(name), which uses similar input validation to build a dynamic greeting, and shout(text), which uppercases the string and adds '!!!'. Only buildGreeting validates input.

bullets.title: No cross-file dependencies or persistent state
bullets.body: All functions operate independently, with no external imports or side effects, so their risk is localized to argument validation and output handling.

callouts.title: Error handling is required at the call site
callouts.body: Both buildGreeting and buildFarewell throw if given invalid input, so applications using these utilities should ensure appropriate error handling to prevent uncaught exceptions.

clusters.name: Cluster A · Add greeting and farewell utility functions

files.riskLabel: Trivial input validation logic in new utility function
files.why: New module exporting buildFarewell() for formatted farewell messages
files.what: This new file adds a function buildFarewell(name), which checks if the input 'name' is a non-empty string and throws an error if not; otherwise, it returns a formatted farewell message. The control flow strictly validates input type and trims whitespace, limiting risk to input misuse. No external dependencies or side effects are introduced.
before: // no equivalent -- this concept did not exist
after: // Second real code change for the two-run diff-pollution exclusion proof
// (PR #559 / opensoyce@0.2.1). This run's diff must show ONLY this file --
// the .opensoyce/walkthroughs/pr-2.json committed by earlier runs must be
// excluded by the CLI's diff-filter, not reviewed as part of this change.
throw new Error('buildFarewell: name must be a non-empty string');
return `Goodbye, ${name.trim()}! Thanks for testing the diff-pollution fix.`;

annos.text: This file introduces buildFarewell(), a function that generates a farewell message string. It first checks if 'name' is a non-empty string (using typeof and .trim()), throwing an error otherwise. If validation passes, it returns a formatted message. This is a new utility; the only logic risk is misuse by passing invalid input, which is caught at runtime.

generationNotes: generator note: cluster badge raised from "lo" to "med" to match risk-list item "Input validation relies on runtime checks in buildFarewell and buildGreeting" (level="med"), which cites this exact file via entity_refs -- the two severity claims about the same file must agree.

files.riskLabel: Basic input validation and string utility functions with minimal risk surface
files.why: Introduces greeting construction and shouting utilities for string manipulation.
files.what: This file exports 'buildGreeting', which constructs a greeting string and throws if provided a non-string or empty name, and 'shout', which uppercases and appends exclamation marks to the provided text. The only input validation is in 'buildGreeting', which verifies the argument is a non-empty string, throwing an error otherwise. There is no further escaping or context-specific sanitization. Both are foundational helpers so risks are limited to misuse or insufficient input validation if called from untrusted sources.
before: // no equivalent -- this concept did not exist
after: // Small utility used by the WIRE-0 external-Action proof PR.
// Deliberately real code (not a docs-only change) so the pr-walkthrough
// pipeline has an actual diff to parse, cluster, and risk-assess.
throw new Error('buildGreeting: name must be a non-empty string');
return `Hello, ${name.trim()}! This walkthrough was generated by the public opensoyce-pr-walkthrough-action.`;

annos.text: 'buildGreeting' ensures its input is a non-empty string, throwing otherwise, and generates a template-based greeting string. 'shout' renders its input (coerced to string) in uppercase with '!!!'. Risk is minimal since both are utility functions, but if inputs are untrusted, dangers like injection or encoding issues may arise in broader contexts.

generationNotes: generator note: cluster badge raised from "lo" to "med" to match risk-list item "Input validation relies on runtime checks in buildFarewell and buildGreeting" (level="med"), which cites this exact file via entity_refs -- the two severity claims about the same file must agree.

risks.title: Input validation relies on runtime checks in buildFarewell and buildGreeting
risks.where: src/farewell.mjs and src/greeting.mjs perform type and presence checks for 'name', throwing an error if the value is not a non-empty string.
risks.what: The buildFarewell and buildGreeting functions check if their input 'name' is a non-empty string using typeof and .trim(); otherwise, they throw an error. This catch-at-runtime approach means that any call site passing user-supplied, potentially malformed, or unexpected values will result in runtime exceptions. If these utilities are called in flows without appropriate error handling, user experience or stability could be impacted.

risks.title: No contextual sanitization or escaping
risks.where: src/greeting.mjs: buildGreeting and shout return string output based solely on provided input, without considering encoding or context-specific escaping.
risks.what: Neither buildGreeting nor shout perform additional escaping or sanitization beyond checking input presence and type. If untrusted data is passed to these functions and their outputs end up in a sensitive context (e.g., HTML, CLI, logs), the calling code remains responsible for any required escaping or defensive handling.

risks.title: No side effects or external dependencies in utility logic
risks.where: Both src/farewell.mjs and src/greeting.mjs define standalone functions with no imports or environmental dependencies.
risks.what: The utility functions as written do not read from or mutate external state, so behavior is predictable given the provided input. As a result, risk is limited to the correctness and appropriateness of argument values at the point of use.

reading: Before this PR, the system had no utility modules for generating greetings or farewells in the codebase; thus, no utility nodes appear.

After the PR, two new utility files are present: src/greeting.mjs and src/farewell.mjs. Both files provide standalone functions for string output (buildGreeting, shout, and buildFarewell), and neither imports or is imported by other modules.

There is no change in relationships with other system components as these new modules function independently. The only new structural links are their existence as part of the utility layer, and their shared lack of dependencies or side effects (risk-2).

nodes.label: Utility Layer (pre-existing)
nodes.sub: No greeting or farewell utilities defined

summary: This change adds two new modules: src/farewell.mjs introduces buildFarewell(), which validates a string input and produces a formatted farewell message, and src/greeting.mjs introduces buildGreeting() (input validation and formatted greeting) and shout() (uppercases text and appends exclamations). The primary risk is that input validation is handled at runtime, so invalid values lead to exceptions if not caught by callers. No external side effects or additional dependencies are introduced.
